scenarios.title: Recall the value two changes before the current one
scenarios.answer:
The timeline of changes is:

1. 99.0% (original)
2. 99.5% (first change)
3. 99.9% (current — updated June 22, 2026)

Two changes before the current one puts you at the original value: **99.0%**.

scenarios.title: Recall the original (first) value after several changes
scenarios.answer: The very first API rate limit was **120 requests per second**. From there it was raised to 300, then 750, and eventually to 2000 rps after a load-balancer upgrade.

scenarios.title: Recall the predecessor entity in a dated rotation
scenarios.answer: The current week starts June 22, 2026 — that's Week 1, with Ana on-call. The week before (the week of June 15–21) would be the previous rotation cycle. Based on the stored schedule, the rotation goes Ana (Week 1) → Ben (Week 2) → Cara (Week 3) → Dmitri (Week 4), cycling back. The week before Week 1 would be Week 4, so **Dmitri** was on-call the week before the current one.

scenarios.title: Recall a metric two updates ago
scenarios.answer:
Based on my stored memory, here's the seat price history I have on record:

- $9/user
- $14/user
- $22/user (current)

Two changes ago would be $14/user.

That said, I also see $35/user appearing in the records, which may represent a different tier or an older/parallel pricing track. If the full sequence is $9 → $14 → $22, then two changes back is $14. Want me to dig deeper into the context around any of those entries?

scenarios.title: Multi-hop join across separate facts (both-pass control)
scenarios.answer:
No, Diego's office day has not started yet.

The payments service is owned by Diego, who is based in the Lisbon office (UTC+0). His workday starts at 09:00 UTC, so an incident at 08:00 UTC would be one hour before his day begins.
